Episode 588: Want Referrals? Don’t Bore Me To Tears

December 12, 2018 by Ivan Misner 10 Comments

Podcast: Download

Subscribe: Apple Podcasts | RSS | Subscription Options

Print This Episode

Synopsis

Charlie Lawson, “unnatural networker” and BNI national director for the UK and Ireland, joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about storytelling, and tells a great story in the process.

What sells your services is not listing what you offer. To give your networking contacts the tools to find you referrals, you need two things:

  1. How does the client feel before you do the work? What’s the situation?
  2. How does the client feel after you do the work?

It’s not important to describe the process. Create an emotional connection between the person you’re talking about and the client. To get an engaging story, tell it from the client’s point of view. Facts tell, stories sell.

To read more of Dr. Misner’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 588 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome to the Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio from Berkeley, CA. And I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. I have a good friend that I’m interviewing with today, Charlie Lawson. Charlie is the Co-National Director of BNI in the UK and Ireland. He is really proud to be a part of developing people, businesses to change lives and enrich communities. But what I think is really interesting is that despite running BNI in the United Kingdom and Ireland, Charlie is, by his own definition, an unnatural networker. He says that most experts talk about networking as though it is easy to talk to strangers, but Charlie is uncomfortable talking to strangers. He helps other unnatural networkers gain confidence by putting networking across the point of view of someone who would rather avoid it completely.

Charlie, it is really a pleasure to have you on BNI Podcast.

Charlie:
Thanks for having me on. I am definitely an unnatural networker, and if I can avoid talking to strangers and chat with my friends and mates instead, then that is much easier. But yeah, it is a nice angle. It is a elephant in the room when I do whisper it.

Ivan:
Yeah, and you know with BNI it’s all about relationships so it is a little bit easier. Your topic for today is “want referrals- don’t bore me to tears.” And I love that title, it is a great title. It’s really about storytelling. So, tell us about storytelling. Why is it so important? It is one of the pieces that we don’t talk enough about, so I’m really happy to have you on. Charlie, go ahead.

Charlie:
I urge everyone to look back at their last chapter meeting and think back on their weekly presentation, were they all captivating and interesting? Did they grab your attention? Some of them I’m sure, but I would imagine not all of them were. If you think back to any networking convention, BNI meeting or somewhere else, how were the conversations there? Were they all engaging? Did they all grab you? Or did you actually find yourself asking, “really do I have to carry on this conversation? Or will I have to be bored to tears?” I think that for me, being an unnatural networker, it’s hard enough for me to talk to strangers in the first place. If I think that I’m boring them to tears, I am very conscious that I want them to be engaged in the conversation. I want to be liked. If an introvert wants to make it, you have got to make it as easy a possible. So, I want to know that people are really listening, and I found that stories are a much better way to do that.

Since we are talking about storytelling, I will share a quick story about Dena. Dena was a BNI member in Yorkshire, in the north of England, and she got a call on a February afternoon at about 5 o’clock- if you have ever been to Yorkshire in February you know by 5 o’clock that it is cold, dark and it was also raining that day. So when Dena answered the phone, she could tell that there was a lady on the other end of the line who was obviously quite upset from the commotion. Dena could hear kids running around and making noise, there was a lot going on. And she said, “What’s going on? How can I help?” Dena ran a listing agency, so she was selling properties to get people put in accommodations- not buying properties but more of a short term, not just an overnight accommodation. What the lady was trying to do- she said, “I’ve got a real problem.” Turns out that she had been chucked out of the house by her partner. Her partner had literally said, “You gotta go to find somewhere else to live.” And left her with the kids. And she was calling Dena to see if there was anything she could do. She said, “I know this doesn’t usually happen at short notice. I know there’s usually contracts and other things of that sort all depending of the availability of the place, but I need somewhere to stay tonight.” Dena managed to source her a property. Not only that, she was able to, in the short term circumvent the process and say, “Hey, look, let’s just get you in the property and we will work out the details tomorrow-“ when it was possible to do it. Not only that, right as she was about to hang up the phone and say, “Look, here’s the details, just come to the property.” She said, “Hang on, where are you?” Turns out that lady had literally been chucked out by her ex partner and was standing out on the roadside with her kids in tow, with a couple of suitcases and boxes, and she had nowhere to go. She had no car. She had no way to get there. So Dena got in her car and said, “I am going to come and pick you up.” She picked them up and took them to the place where they managed to sort everything out. My question for anyone is, would you refer business to Dena?

And I can imagine the answer for most people would be, “Yeah, what an amazing service she offered. She was able to do something short notice- that she may not always be able to do, but she was kind, compassionate, caring and she wanted to help.” What Dena realized was that they were in a real grave situation there and she could make a difference for this lady.

Ivan:
Let me swoop in for just a second here, Charlie. That’s a great story and I want to break it down slightly. What Dena did was establish the setting- and I gotta be honest, what you did with Dena’s story was establish the setting, laid out the crisis and then you described the solution that she gave and how she was able to help and serve that potential customer. That is what makes a great story. Nobody in that room would be bored to tears! I can’t imagine anyone can be bored listening to this podcast. That is. A great example of a great story.

Please go on, Charlie.

Charlie:
When you hear weekly presentations, you hear networking conversations, and it’s often not like that though. It is often- take the listing agent again. A listing agent might be saying at the weekly presentations or networking conversations, “Well I can offer short term leases and last minute solutions. I’ve got a great range of services. I can offer inventory services. I can do this and that.” That doesn’t sell the services of the listing agent. What sells the services of the listing agent is the fact that we know we’d be comfortable referring her. And that is where storytelling happens. It’s not just for making it more engaging and interesting to hear, even though you enjoy what you hear more, it actually makes us do something with it. It becomes memorable. And when it comes memorable, it becomes the sort of thing we bring back up in a future conversation. And next time someone asks me about listing agents, who am I going to think about? I’m going to think about Dena because I have heard that powerful story.

And then I’ll start to break it down and start to think of what’s the difference there. If I am listing off my products and services, effectively that’s just not the information we need to make referrals. We are assuming here that we’ve got a good relationship. Once we’ve got a good relationship- you know that is a prerequisite for networking obviously, you have got to have a relationship there. But once we’ve got a relationship, we want to give our networking contacts the tools to find those referrals. And just listing our products and services doesn’t work. What we really need to figure out is how did the client feel before we did the work? What’s the situation they are in, and how are they feeling about life? You know, generally in that situation, there is going to be some level of emotion, so be careful here. Often when we think of emotion we think of sadness because that’s an obvious emotion, but there are all sorts of emotions; worry, anxious, anger, happiness- all sorts of different emotions. Let’s take Dena’s client for an example. How was she feeling? Well, she was feeling scared, very worried, she’s got kids running around. Imagine how she is feeling. That’s a horrible situation to be in. And what we have got to try to do is when we are telling our stories, is putting it across from the client’s point of view so their emotion comes through. That’s when you get the visceral connection to a story. Even though we may not have been in that situation ourselves and of course that is quite an extreme story, but it’s the visceral connection that makes us feel and be like, “That poor lady! What happened? How does the story end?” Then we don’t need to hear what Dena actually did. In terms of the due process, she actually did get the lady into a flat- but we do not really care about that. What we really care after that is, how did the person feel afterwards?

I’ll refer to my friend Andy Bounds. He’s spoken at BNI events in the UK, Ireland and other countries as well. He is a sales guy and messaging guy, and talks about the ‘after’. You know, you don’t buy a product for the product. You buy a product for what the product gives you. I’ll try to give an example: You don’t buy a pair of glasses for the glasses, you buy them for sight. The sight is the ‘after’ that we like. So, the ‘after’ in this case is how did the lady feel afterwards? What was the situation’s result? You can then feel comfortable to refer them.

When you are focusing on stories in your weekly presentation and your networking conversation, think about it from the client’s point of view, not your point of view. When you are thinking from your point of view, you are just sort of trying to say what you did. Imagine if I told Dena’s story like, “I received a call and I realized I had to get somewhere last minute, and I had to look up a list of accommodations to find this out…“ It’s just not that interesting. When it’s from a clients point of view, we get an engaging story. We don’t bore people to tears. Also, people start to do something with it.

Ivan:
Well, we are almost out of time, and I want to give you a moment to wrap up. Before I do, I just want to repeat for everyone who is listening, two key factors that Charlie just shared for everyone. Number one, in your networking meeting, how do they feel before you turned up? In your presentation you want to describe how people felt before you turned up. And then, you want to talk about how they feel after you did the work. Those two things are key and I am really happy that you are on this podcast to share with us, Charlie.

Any closing thoughts to leave us with before we wrap up?

Charlie:
Yeah, the moral of the story is that I think storytelling is more interesting, memorable, and crucially more referable. And that is why we go to networking events and BNI because we want to build our business through referrals. We’ve got to give our fellow members the tools to find those referrals, and I think storytelling is a great way to do it. Plus, it won’t bore people to tears when it comes to your networking.

Ivan:
I love it, Charlie. Everyone who is listening to this, you have to share this with members of your chapter. Education Coordinators, this is a great educational slot. Share this content with your BNI chapters. Remember, facts tell, stories sell. And stories are what give that visceral feel that you were talking about, Charlie.

Thanks so much for being on the BNI podcast. It was a pleasure having you on.

Charlie:
It was my pleasure.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, well thank you both very much. I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much for the great information. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

Special Guest Charlie Lawson Posted in Referrals, Special Guests

Comments

    Solutions-focused comments are welcome. Dr. Misner reads every comment personally.

    All comments are moderated before posting. Profanity, personal attacks, and sales pitches will be deleted.

  1. Mark Appelbaum says

    December 12, 2018 at 7:03 pm

    Great podcast. Behind every interaction/transaction there is a story, something unique about the person you’re working with. If more members simply started getting in the habit of sharing those stories, their results would probably improve. Thank you Charlie for sharing this.

    Reply

  2. Jerry Vogt says

    December 12, 2018 at 8:23 pm

    Well put for the shy ones amongst us

    Reply

  3. Thom Little says

    December 12, 2018 at 10:52 pm

    Well done … with podcast 588 you just posted your 500th podcast … congratulations.

    … Thom

    Reply

  4. Steve Standish Asbestos Removal Specialist says

    December 13, 2018 at 9:32 pm

    Great pod cast. I will use stories as part of my BNI presentation as they are a type of testimonial as well. Thanks Steve

    Reply

  5. Laura Reid says

    December 16, 2018 at 7:12 pm

    I was thrilled to listen to this lasted podcast – thank you for addressing the power of storytelling! I am a BNI member in Waimea, Big Island, Hawaii and recently launched a new business as a “Story Strategist” dedicated to teaching storytelling to business owners and leaders!

    You did not bore me to tears and I will be reaching out to you Charlie!

    Aloha, Laura

    Reply

  6. Mack McCoy says

    December 18, 2018 at 2:01 am

    The dirty little secret is that many of us . . . don’t have a story to tell. We don’t know why our product or service is of value. Many of us got into our profession, not because we wanted to help or serve people, but because we thought we could make an easy dollar of it!

    At the same time, many of us DO have a story to tell, but we don’t know how to tell it. Maybe we’re afraid of exposing too much of ourselves, maybe we have a “false modesty” that inhibits us in explaining the “why.”

    I knew a typesetter once – not super old school, but it was the ’80s – and she had a “why”: I love reading, and good typography makes it easier for me to read. If I can help graphic designers by giving them good typography, then their audience will find it easier to read. Nothing profound, nothing Gandhi-like, just simply, if I put my skills to use, it will make some little thing better.

    Reply

  7. Jay Johnson says

    January 3, 2019 at 12:18 pm

    I love this, “facts tell, stories sell.”

    Reply

  8. Sally Onesty says

    January 11, 2019 at 10:51 pm

    When you create an emotional connection between the person you’re talking about and the client you’re already creating an experience. Every time you share an engaging story, tell it from the client’s point of view. Facts tell; stories sell.

    Reply

  9. Marilyn Brennan says

    January 14, 2019 at 12:41 pm

    good podcast, remember to tell a story!

    Reply

    • Cosby Bikpe says

      January 27, 2023 at 1:43 am

      Awesome podcast. Thanks BNI

      Reply
